query:
You are working on the Kotlin/kotlinx-datetime repository.
The codebase is at version 0.6 (commit: e721269c).

## Issue to Resolve

API reference improvements
It would be nice to systematically and extensively improve our API reference:

For every API endpoint, it would be nice to provide the following (example is the place where the property does not hold):

* Extensively document what the entity represents, whether it follows ISO-8601, **how the entity can be acquired**, and what time scale it comes from. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-day-of-week/) 
* Provide a minimum of how the entity can be used. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-fixed-offset-time-zone/)
* Thoroughly explain the allowed and expected contract and behaviours and system-specifics. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-clock/)
* If applicable, provide contexts in which the entity is expected/recommended to be used (e.g. our `README` shines at that) and how it interacts with other (core) entities. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-local-date-time/)
* Provide a basic usage example. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-time-zone/)
* If applicable, provide details about conversion rules, gotchas and recommendations. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-instant/)
* Where appropriate, information about conversions and interactions with native types (e.g. Instant <-> Java Instant conversion)
* Ensure that information used in our `README` is, in fact, deducible from the core entities documentation

## Additional Context from Issue Discussion

> If applicable, provide details about conversion rules, gotchas and recommendations. [Example](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-instant/)

I don't know of gotchas related to `Instant`. The only one that comes to mind is that `instant + 1.days` is a non-calendar operation (this could be worth documenting in the `plus` function), but I'd argue it's `Duration`'s gotcha. `Instant` seems tough to use incorrectly to me. What gotchas did you have in mind?

> Where appropriate, information about conversions and interactions with native types (e.g. Instant <-> Java Instant conversion)

When you have specific behavior on some platforms, you document it in the common code, makes sense; but when you have some extra interactions with platform code, you can't document them in common code (because symbols aren't resolved). If you have platform-specific documentation, in the IDE, it is either ignored (when writing common code) or replaces the common documentation. In the latter case, you have to repeat the common documentation for platforms *and* include platform-specific additions, which I think just isn't worth it.

If the system was a bit different and platform-specific documentation was *added* to the common documentation (and maybe the IDE would be smart enough to show the documentation for the platforms the project actually uses), then I agree that mentioning platform-specific converters and so on would be beneficial. Or better yet, if there was "extension documentation" along with extension functions: then we'd be able to show `NSDate` conversions in the `Instant` documentation for Darwin targets.

## Relevant Files

The following files are likely relevant to solving this issue:
- `README.md`
- `core/build.gradle.kts`
- `core/common/src/Clock.kt`
- `core/common/src/DateTimePeriod.kt`
- `core/common/src/DateTimeUnit.kt`
- `core/common/src/DayOfWeek.kt`
- `core/common/src/Exceptions.kt`
- `core/common/src/Instant.kt`
- `core/common/src/LocalDate.kt`
- `core/common/src/LocalDateTime.kt`
- `core/common/src/LocalTime.kt`
- `core/common/src/Month.kt`
- `core/common/src/TimeZone.kt`
- `core/common/src/UtcOffset.kt`
- `core/common/src/format/DateTimeComponents.kt`
- `core/common/src/format/DateTimeFormat.kt`
- `core/common/src/format/DateTimeFormatBuilder.kt`
- `core/common/src/format/LocalDateFormat.kt`
- `core/common/src/format/Unicode.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/DateTimePeriodSerializers.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/InstantSerializers.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/LocalDateSerializers.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/LocalDateTimeSerializers.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/LocalTimeSerializers.kt`
- `core/common/src/serializers/TimeZoneSerializers.kt`
- `core/commonJs/src/TimeZone.kt`
- `core/commonJs/src/UtcOffset.kt`
- `core/jvm/src/TimeZoneJvm.kt`
- `core/jvm/src/UtcOffsetJvm.kt`
- `core/native/src/TimeZone.kt`
- `core/native/src/UtcOffset.kt`

## Instructions

Please analyze this issue and implement a fix. Make the necessary code changes to resolve the problem described above.

Focus on:
- Understanding the root cause of the issue
- Making minimal, targeted changes to fix the problem
- Ensuring your changes don't break existing functionality

## Important Evaluation Notes

**Your work will be evaluated by running hidden tests that you do not have access to.** These tests will verify that your fix correctly addresses the issue. You should be confident in your solution before finishing.
**If you write tests to verify your changes, create them in NEW test files only** - do NOT modify existing test files

## CRITICAL RESTRICTIONS

**NEVER use git commands.** Do not run `git status`, `git diff`, `git checkout`, `git reset`, or ANY other git commands. Using git will corrupt the evaluation environment and invalidate your entire submission. All file operations should be done through normal file editing and reading tools, not git.

1â†’# kotlinx-datetime
  2â†’
  3â†’[![Kotlin Alpha](https://kotl.in/badges/alpha.svg)](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/components-stability.html)
  4â†’[![JetBrains official project](https://jb.gg/badges/official.svg)](https://confluence.jetbrains.com/display/ALL/JetBrains+on+GitHub) 
  5â†’[![GitHub license](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-Apache%20License%202.0-blue.svg?style=flat)](http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
  6â†’[![Maven Central](https://img.shields.io/maven-central/v/org.jetbrains.kotlinx/kotlinx-datetime.svg?label=Maven%20Central)](https://search.maven.org/search?q=g:%22org.jetbrains.kotlinx%22%20AND%20a:%22kotlinx-datetime%22)
  7â†’[![Kotlin](https://img.shields.io/badge/kotlin-1.9.21-blue.svg?logo=kotlin)](http://kotlinlang.org)
  8â†’[![KDoc link](https://img.shields.io/badge/API_reference-KDoc-blue)](https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/)
  9â†’[![Slack channel](https://img.shields.io/badge/chat-slack-blue.svg?logo=slack)](https://kotlinlang.slack.com/messages/kotlinx-datetime/)
 10â†’[![TeamCity build](https://img.shields.io/teamcity/build/s/KotlinTools_KotlinxDatetime_Build_All.svg?server=http%3A%2F%2Fteamcity.jetbrains.com)](https://teamcity.jetbrains.com/viewType.html?buildTypeId=KotlinTools_KotlinxDatetime_Build_All&guest=1)
 11â†’
 12â†’A multiplatform Kotlin library for working with date and time.
 13â†’
 14â†’See [Using in your projects](#using-in-your-projects) for the instructions how to setup a dependency in your project.
 15â†’
 16â†’## Design overview
 17â†’
 18â†’There are a few guiding principles in the design of `kotlinx-datetime`. First of all, it is pragmatic, focused
 19â†’on the most common problems developers face every day (pun intended) when working with dates and times. It is not
 20â†’all-encompassing and lacks some domain-specific utilities that special-purpose applications might need.
 21â†’We chose convenience over generality, so the API surface this library provides is as minimal as possible
 22â†’to meet the use-cases.
 23â†’
 24â†’The library puts a clear boundary between the physical time of an instant and the local, time-zone dependent 
 25â†’civil time, consisting of components such as year, month, etc that people use when talking about time. 
 26â†’We intentionally avoid entities in the library that mix both together and could be misused.
 27â†’However, there are convenience operations that take, for example, a physical instant and perform a calendar-based 
 28â†’adjustment (such as adding a month); all such operations
 29â†’explicitly take a time-zone information as parameter to clearly state that their result depends on the civil time-zone
 30â†’rules which are subject to change at any time.
 31â†’
 32â†’The library is based on the ISO 8601 international standard, other ways to represent dates and times are out of
 33â†’its scope. Internationalization (such as locale-specific month and day names) is out the scope, too. 
 34â†’
 35â†’## Types
 36â†’
 37â†’The library provides a basic set of types for working with date and time:
 38â†’
 39â†’- `Instant` to represent a moment on the UTC-SLS time scale;
 40â†’- `Clock` to obtain the current instant;
 41â†’- `LocalDateTime` to represent date and time components without a reference to the particular time zone; 
 42â†’- `LocalDate` to represent the components of date only;
 43â†’- `LocalTime` to represent the components of time only;
 44â†’- `TimeZone` and `FixedOffsetTimeZone` provide time zone information to convert between `Instant` and `LocalDateTime`;
 45â†’- `Month` and `DayOfWeek` enums;
 46â†’- `DateTimePeriod` to represent a difference between two instants decomposed into date and time units;
 47â†’- `DatePeriod` is a subclass of `DateTimePeriod` with zero time components,
 48â†’it represents a difference between two LocalDate values decomposed into date units.
 49â†’- `DateTimeUnit` provides a set of predefined date and time units to use in arithmetic operations on `Instant` and `LocalDate`. 
 50â†’- `UtcOffset` represents the amount of time the local date/time at a particular time zone differs from the date/time at UTC.
 51â†’
 52â†’### Type use-cases
 53â†’
 54â†’Here is some basic advice on how to choose which of the date-carrying types to use in what cases:
 55â†’
 56â†’- Use `Instant` to represent a timestamp of the event that had already happened in the past (like a timestamp of 
 57â†’  a log entry) or will definitely happen in a well-defined instant of time in the future not far away from now 
 58â†’  (like an order confirmation deadline in 1 hour from now).
 59â†’  
 60â†’- Use `LocalDateTime` to represent a time of the event that is scheduled to happen in the far future at a certain 
 61â†’  local time (like a scheduled meeting in a few months from now). You'll have to keep track of the `TimeZone` of 
 62â†’  the scheduled event separately. Try to avoid converting future events to `Instant` in advance, because time-zone 
 63â†’  rules might change unexpectedly in the future. In this [blog post](https://codeblog.jonskeet.uk/2019/03/27/storing-utc-is-not-a-silver-bullet/), you can read more about why it's not always 
 64â†’  a good idea to use `Instant` everywhere.
 65â†’  
 66â†’  Also, use `LocalDateTime` to decode an `Instant` to its local date-time components for display and UIs.
 67â†’  
 68â†’- Use `LocalDate` to represent the date of an event that does not have a specific time associated with it (like a birth date).
 69â†’
 70â†’- Use `LocalTime` to represent the time of an event that does not have a specific date associated with it.
 71â†’ 
 72â†’## Operations
 73â†’
 74â†’With the above types you can get the following operations done.
 75â†’
 76â†’
 77â†’### Getting the current moment of time
 78â†’
 79â†’The current moment of time can be captured with the `Instant` type. 
 80â†’To obtain an `Instant` corresponding to the current moment of time, 
 81â†’use `now()` function of the `Clock` interface:
 82â†’
 83â†’```kotlin
 84â†’val clock: Clock = ...
 85â†’val currentMoment = clock.now()
 86â†’```
 87â†’
 88â†’An instance of `Clock` can be injected through the function/class parameters, 
 89â†’or you can use its default implementation `Clock.System` that represents the system clock:
 90â†’
 91â†’```kotlin
 92â†’val currentMoment = Clock.System.now()
 93â†’```
 94â†’
 95â†’
 96â†’### Converting an instant to local date and time components
 97â†’
 98â†’An `Instant` is just a counter of high resolution time intervals since the beginning of time scale.
 99â†’To get human readable components from an `Instant` value, you need to convert it to the `LocalDateTime`
100â†’type that represents date and time components without a reference to the particular time zone.
101â†’
102â†’The `TimeZone` type provides the rules to convert instants from and to date/time components.
103â†’
104â†’```kotlin
105â†’val currentMoment: Instant = Clock.System.now()
106â†’val datetimeInUtc: LocalDateTime = currentMoment.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.UTC)
107â†’val datetimeInSystemZone: LocalDateTime = currentMoment.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
108â†’```
109â†’
110â†’A `LocalDateTime` instance exposes familiar components of the Gregorian calendar: 
111â†’`year`, `month`, `dayOfMonth`, `hour`, and so on up to `nanosecond`.
112â†’The property `dayOfWeek` shows what weekday that date is,
113â†’and `dayOfYear` shows the day number since the beginning of a year.
114â†’
115â†’
116â†’Additional time zones can be acquired by their string identifier with the `TimeZone.of(id: String)` function.
117â†’```kotlin
118â†’val tzBerlin = TimeZone.of("Europe/Berlin")
119â†’val datetimeInBerlin = currentMoment.toLocalDateTime(tzBerlin)
120â†’```
121â†’
122â†’A `LocalDateTime` instance can be constructed from individual components:
123â†’
124â†’```kotlin
125â†’val kotlinReleaseDateTime = LocalDateTime(2016, 2, 15, 16, 57, 0, 0)
126â†’```
127â†’
128â†’An instant can be obtained from `LocalDateTime` by interpreting it as a time moment
129â†’in a particular `TimeZone`:
130â†’
131â†’```kotlin
132â†’val kotlinReleaseInstant = kotlinReleaseDateTime.toInstant(TimeZone.of("UTC+3"))
133â†’```
134â†’
135â†’### Getting local date components
136â†’
137â†’A `LocalDate` represents a local date without time. You can obtain one from an `Instant`
138â†’by converting it to `LocalDateTime` and taking its `date` property.
139â†’
140â†’```kotlin
141â†’val now: Instant = Clock.System.now()
142â†’val today: LocalDate = now.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()).date
143â†’// or shorter
144â†’val today: LocalDate = Clock.System.todayIn(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
145â†’```
146â†’Note, that today's date really depends on the time zone in which you're observing the current moment.
147â†’
148â†’`LocalDate` can be constructed from three components, year, month, and day:
149â†’```kotlin
150â†’val knownDate = LocalDate(2020, 2, 21)
151â†’```
152â†’
153â†’### Getting local time components
154â†’
155â†’A `LocalTime` represents local time without date. You can obtain one from an `Instant`
156â†’by converting it to `LocalDateTime` and taking its `time` property.
157â†’
158â†’```kotlin
159â†’val now: Instant = Clock.System.now()
160â†’val thisTime: LocalTime = now.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()).time
161â†’```
162â†’
163â†’A `LocalTime` can be constructed from four components, hour, minute, second and nanosecond:
164â†’```kotlin
165â†’val knownTime = LocalTime(hour = 23, minute = 59, second = 12)
166â†’val timeWithNanos = LocalTime(hour = 23, minute = 59, second = 12, nanosecond = 999)
167â†’val hourMinute = LocalTime(hour = 12, minute = 13)
168â†’```
169â†’
170â†’### Converting instant to and from unix time
171â†’
172â†’An `Instant` can be converted to a number of milliseconds since the Unix/POSIX epoch with the `toEpochMilliseconds()` function.
173â†’To convert back, use the companion object function `Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds(Long)`.
174â†’
175â†’### Converting instant and local date/time to and from the ISO 8601 string
176â†’
177â†’`Instant`, `LocalDateTime`, `LocalDate` and `LocalTime` provide shortcuts for
178â†’parsing and formatting them using the extended ISO-8601 format.
179â†’The `toString()` function is used to convert the value to a string in that format, and 
180â†’the `parse` function in companion object is used to parse a string representation back. 
181â†’
182â†’```kotlin
183â†’val instantNow = Clock.System.now()
184â†’instantNow.toString()  // returns something like 2015-12-31T12:30:00Z
185â†’val instantBefore = Instant.parse("2010-06-01T22:19:44.475Z")
186â†’```
187â†’
188â†’`LocalDateTime` uses a similar format, but without `Z` UTC time zone designator in the end.
189â†’
190â†’`LocalDate` uses a format with just year, month, and date components, e.g. `2010-06-01`.
191â†’
192â†’`LocalTime` uses a format with just hour, minute, second and (if non-zero) nanosecond components, e.g. `12:01:03`.
193â†’
194â†’```kotlin
195â†’LocalDateTime.parse("2010-06-01T22:19:44")
196â†’LocalDate.parse("2010-06-01")
197â†’LocalTime.parse("12:01:03")
198â†’LocalTime.parse("12:00:03.999")
199â†’LocalTime.parse("12:0:03.999") // fails with an IllegalArgumentException
200â†’```

conversation_history.content:
1â†’/*
  2â†’ * Copyright 2019-2020 JetBrains s.r.o.
  3â†’ * Use of this source code is governed by the Apache 2.0 License that can be found in the LICENSE.txt file.
  4â†’ */
  5â†’
  6â†’package kotlinx.datetime
  7â†’
  8â†’import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
  9â†’import kotlinx.datetime.internal.*
 10â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.InstantIso8601Serializer
 11â†’import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 12â†’import kotlin.time.*
 13â†’
 14â†’/**
 15â†’ * A moment in time.
 16â†’ *
 17â†’ * A point in time must be uniquely identified, so that it is independent of a time zone.
 18â†’ * For example, `1970-01-01, 00:00:00` does not represent a moment in time, since this would happen at different times
 19â†’ * in different time zones: someone in Tokyo would think its already `1970-01-01` several hours earlier than someone in
 20â†’ * Berlin would. To represent such entities, use [LocalDateTime].
 21â†’ * In contrast, "the moment the clocks in London first showed 00:00 on Jan 1, 2000" is a specific moment
 22â†’ * in time, as is "1970-01-01, 00:00:00 UTC+0", and so it can be represented as an [Instant].
 23â†’ *
 24â†’ * `Instant` uses the UTC-SLS (smeared leap second) time scale. This time scale doesn't contain instants
 25â†’ * corresponding to leap seconds, but instead "smears" positive and negative leap seconds among the last 1000 seconds
 26â†’ * of the day when a leap second happens.
 27â†’ *
 28â†’ * Some ways in which [Instant] can be acquired are:
 29â†’ * - [Clock.now] can be used to query the current moment for the given clock. With [Clock.System], it is the current
 30â†’ *   moment as the platform sees it.
 31â†’ * - [Instant.parse] parses an ISO-8601 string.
 32â†’ * - [Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds] and [Instant.fromEpochSeconds] construct the instant values from the amount of time
 33â†’ *   since `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` (the Unix epoch).
 34â†’ */
 35â†’@Serializable(with = InstantIso8601Serializer::class)
 36â†’public expect class Instant : Comparable<Instant> {
 37â†’
 38â†’    /**
 39â†’     * The number of seconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` rounded down to a [Long] number.
 40â†’     *
 41â†’     * The difference between the rounded number of seconds and the actual number of seconds
 42â†’     * is returned by [nanosecondsOfSecond] property expressed in nanoseconds.
 43â†’     *
 44â†’     * Note that this number doesn't include leap seconds added or removed since the epoch.
 45â†’     *
 46â†’     * @see Instant.fromEpochSeconds
 47â†’     */
 48â†’    public val epochSeconds: Long
 49â†’
 50â†’    /**
 51â†’     * The number of nanoseconds by which this instant is later than [epochSeconds] from the epoch instant.
 52â†’     *
 53â†’     * The value is always positive and lies in the range `0..999_999_999`.
 54â†’     *
 55â†’     * @see Instant.fromEpochSeconds
 56â†’     */
 57â†’    public val nanosecondsOfSecond: Int
 58â†’
 59â†’    /**
 60â†’     * Returns the number of milliseconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`.
 61â†’     *
 62â†’     * Any fractional part of millisecond is rounded down to the whole number of milliseconds.
 63â†’     *
 64â†’     * If the result does not fit in [Long], returns [Long.MAX_VALUE] for a positive result or [Long.MIN_VALUE] for a negative result.
 65â†’     *
 66â†’     * @see Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds
 67â†’     */
 68â†’    public fun toEpochMilliseconds(): Long
 69â†’
 70â†’    /**
 71â†’     * Returns an instant that is the result of adding the specified [duration] to this instant.
 72â†’     *
 73â†’     * If the [duration] is positive, the returned instant is later than this instant.
 74â†’     * If the [duration] is negative, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
 75â†’     *
 76â†’     * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
 77â†’     */
 78â†’    public operator fun plus(duration: Duration): Instant
 79â†’
 80â†’    /**
 81â†’     * Returns an instant that is the result of subtracting the specified [duration] from this instant.
 82â†’     *
 83â†’     * If the [duration] is positive, the returned instant is earlier than this instant.
 84â†’     * If the [duration] is negative, the returned instant is later than this instant.
 85â†’     *
 86â†’     * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
 87â†’     */
 88â†’    public operator fun minus(duration: Duration): Instant
 89â†’
 90â†’    // questionable
 91â†’    /**
 92â†’     * Returns the [Duration] between two instants: [other] and `this`.
 93â†’     *
 94â†’     * The duration returned is positive if this instant is later than the other,
 95â†’     * and negative if this instant is earlier than the other.
 96â†’     *
 97â†’     * The result is never clamped, but note that for instants that are far apart,
 98â†’     * the value returned may represent the duration between them inexactly due to the loss of precision.
 99â†’     */
100â†’    public operator fun minus(other: Instant): Duration
101â†’
102â†’    /**
103â†’     * Compares `this` instant with the [other] instant.
104â†’     * Returns zero if this instant represents the same moment as the other (i.e. equal to other),
105â†’     * a negative number if this instant is earlier than the other,
106â†’     * and a positive number if this instant is later than the other.
107â†’     */
108â†’    public override operator fun compareTo(other: Instant): Int
109â†’
110â†’    /**
111â†’     * Converts this instant to the ISO-8601 string representation.
112â†’     *
113â†’     * The representation uses the UTC-SLS time scale, instead of UTC.
114â†’     * In practice, this means that leap second handling will not be readjusted to the UTC.
115â†’     * Leap seconds will not be added or skipped, so it is impossible to acquire a string
116â†’     * where the component for seconds is 60, and for any day, it's possible to observe 23:59:59.
117â†’     *
118â†’     * @see Instant.parse
119â†’     * @see DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET for a very similar format. The difference is that
120â†’     * [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] will not add trailing zeros for readability to the
121â†’     * fractional part of the second.
122â†’     */
123â†’    public override fun toString(): String
124â†’
125â†’
126â†’    public companion object {
127â†’        @Deprecated("Use Clock.System.now() instead", ReplaceWith("Clock.System.now()", "kotlinx.datetime.Clock"), level = DeprecationLevel.ERROR)
128â†’        public fun now(): Instant
129â†’
130â†’        /**
131â†’         * Returns an [Instant] that is [epochMilliseconds] number of milliseconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`.
132â†’         *
133â†’         * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
134â†’         *
135â†’         * @see Instant.toEpochMilliseconds
136â†’         */
137â†’        public fun fromEpochMilliseconds(epochMilliseconds: Long): Instant
138â†’
139â†’        /**
140â†’         * Returns an [Instant] that is the [epochSeconds] number of seconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`
141â†’         * and the [nanosecondAdjustment] number of nanoseconds from the whole second.
142â†’         *
143â†’         * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
144â†’         */
145â†’        public fun fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds: Long, nanosecondAdjustment: Long = 0): Instant
146â†’
147â†’        /**
148â†’         * Returns an [Instant] that is the [epochSeconds] number of seconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z`
149â†’         * and the [nanosecondAdjustment] number of nanoseconds from the whole second.
150â†’         *
151â†’         * The return value is clamped to the platform-specific boundaries for [Instant] if the result exceeds them.
152â†’         */
153â†’        public fun fromEpochSeconds(epochSeconds: Long, nanosecondAdjustment: Int): Instant
154â†’
155â†’        /**
156â†’         * A shortcut for calling [DateTimeFormat.parse], followed by [DateTimeComponents.toInstantUsingOffset].
157â†’         *
158â†’         * Parses a string that represents an instant including date and time components and a mandatory
159â†’         * time zone offset and returns the parsed [Instant] value.
160â†’         *
161â†’         * The string is considered to represent time on the UTC-SLS time scale instead of UTC.
162â†’         * In practice, this means that, even if there is a leap second on the given day, it will not affect how the
163â†’         * time is parsed, even if it's in the last 1000 seconds of the day.
164â†’         * Instead, even if there is a negative leap second on the given day, 23:59:59 is still considered valid time.
165â†’         * 23:59:60 is invalid on UTC-SLS, so parsing it will fail.
166â†’         *
167â†’         * If the format is not specified, [DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET] is used.
168â†’         *
169â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [Instant] are exceeded.
170â†’         *
171â†’         * @see Instant.toString for formatting using the default format.
172â†’         * @see Instant.format for formatting using a custom format.
173â†’         */
174â†’        public fun parse(
175â†’            input: CharSequence,
176â†’            format: DateTimeFormat<DateTimeComponents> = DateTimeComponents.Formats.ISO_DATE_TIME_OFFSET
177â†’        ): Instant
178â†’
179â†’        /**
180â†’         * An instant value that is far in the past.
181â†’         *
182â†’         * All instants in the range `DISTANT_PAST..DISTANT_FUTURE` can be [converted][Instant.toLocalDateTime] to
183â†’         * [LocalDateTime] without exceptions on all supported platforms.
184â†’         */
185â†’        public val DISTANT_PAST: Instant // -100001-12-31T23:59:59.999999999Z
186â†’
187â†’        /**
188â†’         * An instant value that is far in the future.
189â†’         *
190â†’         * All instants in the range `DISTANT_PAST..DISTANT_FUTURE` can be [converted][Instant.toLocalDateTime] to
191â†’         * [LocalDateTime] without exceptions on all supported platforms.
192â†’         */
193â†’        public val DISTANT_FUTURE: Instant // +100000-01-01T00:00:00Z
194â†’
195â†’        internal val MIN: Instant
196â†’        internal val MAX: Instant
197â†’    }
198â†’}
199â†’
200â†’/** Returns true if the instant is [Instant.DISTANT_PAST] or earlier. */

  6â†’@file:kotlin.jvm.JvmMultifileClass
  7â†’@file:kotlin.jvm.JvmName("TimeZoneKt")
  8â†’
  9â†’package kotlinx.datetime
 10â†’
 11â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.*
 12â†’import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 13â†’
 14â†’/**
 15â†’ * A time zone, provides the conversion between [Instant] and [LocalDateTime] values
 16â†’ * using a collection of rules specifying which [LocalDateTime] value corresponds to each [Instant].
 17â†’ */
 18â†’@Serializable(with = TimeZoneSerializer::class)
 19â†’public expect open class TimeZone {
 20â†’    /**
 21â†’     * Returns the identifier string of the time zone.
 22â†’     *
 23â†’     * This identifier can be used later for finding this time zone with [TimeZone.of] function.
 24â†’     */
 25â†’    public val id: String
 26â†’
 27â†’    // TODO: Declare and document toString/equals/hashCode
 28â†’
 29â†’    public companion object {
 30â†’        /**
 31â†’         * Queries the current system time zone.
 32â†’         *
 33â†’         * If the current system time zone changes, this function can reflect this change on the next invocation.
 34â†’         */
 35â†’        public fun currentSystemDefault(): TimeZone
 36â†’
 37â†’        /**
 38â†’         * Returns the time zone with the fixed UTC+0 offset.
 39â†’         */
 40â†’        public val UTC: FixedOffsetTimeZone
 41â†’
 42â†’        /**
 43â†’         * Returns the time zone identified by the provided [zoneId].
 44â†’         *
 45â†’         * The supported variants of time zone identifiers:
 46â†’         * - `Z`, 'UTC', 'UT' or 'GMT' â€” identifies the fixed-offset time zone [TimeZone.UTC],
 47â†’         * - a string starting with '+', '-', `UTC+`, `UTC-`, `UT+`, `UT-`, `GMT+`, `GMT-` â€” identifiers the time zone
 48â†’         *   with the fixed offset specified after `+` or `-`,
 49â†’         * - all other strings are treated as region-based zone identifiers.
 50â†’         * In the IANA Time Zone Database (TZDB) which is used as the default source of time zones,
 51â†’         * these ids are usually in the form `area/city`, for example, `Europe/Berlin` or `America/Los_Angeles`.
 52â†’         *
 53â†’         * @throws IllegalTimeZoneException if [zoneId] has an invalid format or a time-zone with the name [zoneId]
 54â†’         * is not found.
 55â†’         */
 56â†’        public fun of(zoneId: String): TimeZone
 57â†’
 58â†’        /**
 59â†’         * Queries the set of identifiers of time zones available in the system.
 60â†’         */
 61â†’        public val availableZoneIds: Set<String>
 62â†’    }
 63â†’
 64â†’    /**
 65â†’     * Return the civil date/time value that this instant has in the time zone provided as an implicit receiver.
 66â†’     *
 67â†’     * Note that while this conversion is unambiguous, the inverse ([LocalDateTime.toInstant])
 68â†’     * is not necessary so.
 69â†’     *
 70â†’     * @see LocalDateTime.toInstant
 71â†’     * @see Instant.offsetIn
 72â†’     * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
 73â†’     */
 74â†’    public fun Instant.toLocalDateTime(): LocalDateTime
 75â†’
 76â†’    /**
 77â†’     * Returns an instant that corresponds to this civil date/time value in the time zone provided as an implicit receiver.
 78â†’     *
 79â†’     * Note that the conversion is not always unambiguous. There can be the following possible situations:
 80â†’     * - There's only one instant that has this date/time value in the time zone. In this case
 81â†’     * the conversion is unambiguous.
 82â†’     * - There's no instant that has this date/time value in the time zone. Such situation appears when
 83â†’     * the time zone experiences a transition from a lesser to a greater offset. In this case the conversion is performed with
 84â†’     * the lesser offset.
 85â†’     * - There are two possible instants that can have this date/time components in the time zone. In this case the earlier
 86â†’     * instant is returned.
 87â†’     *
 88â†’     * @see Instant.toLocalDateTime
 89â†’     */
 90â†’    public fun LocalDateTime.toInstant(): Instant
 91â†’}
 92â†’
 93â†’/**
 94â†’ * A time zone that is known to always have the same offset from UTC.
 95â†’ */
 96â†’@Serializable(with = FixedOffsetTimeZoneSerializer::class)
 97â†’public expect class FixedOffsetTimeZone : TimeZone {
 98â†’    public constructor(offset: UtcOffset)
 99â†’
100â†’    /**
101â†’     * The constant offset from UTC that this time zone has.
102â†’     */
103â†’    public val offset: UtcOffset
104â†’
105â†’    @Deprecated("Use offset.totalSeconds", ReplaceWith("offset.totalSeconds"))
106â†’    public val totalSeconds: Int
107â†’}
108â†’
109â†’@Deprecated("Use FixedOffsetTimeZone of UtcOffset instead", ReplaceWith("FixedOffsetTimeZone"))
110â†’public typealias ZoneOffset = FixedOffsetTimeZone
111â†’
112â†’/**
113â†’ * Finds the offset from UTC this time zone has at the specified [instant] of physical time.
114â†’ *
115â†’ * @see Instant.toLocalDateTime
116â†’ * @see TimeZone.offsetAt
117â†’ */
118â†’public expect fun TimeZone.offsetAt(instant: Instant): UtcOffset
119â†’
120â†’/**
121â†’ * Returns a civil date/time value that this instant has in the specified [timeZone].
122â†’ *
123â†’ * Note that while this conversion is unambiguous, the inverse ([LocalDateTime.toInstant])
124â†’ * is not necessary so.
125â†’ *
126â†’ * @see LocalDateTime.toInstant
127â†’ * @see Instant.offsetIn
128â†’ * @throws DateTimeArithmeticException if this value is too large to fit in [LocalDateTime].
129â†’ */
130â†’public expect fun Instant.toLocalDateTime(timeZone: TimeZone): LocalDateTime
131â†’
132â†’/**
133â†’ * Returns a civil date/time value that this instant has in the specified [UTC offset][offset].
134â†’ *
135â†’ * @see LocalDateTime.toInstant
136â†’ * @see Instant.offsetIn
137â†’ */
138â†’internal expect fun Instant.toLocalDateTime(offset: UtcOffset): LocalDateTime
139â†’
140â†’/**
141â†’ * Finds the offset from UTC the specified [timeZone] has at this instant of physical time.
142â†’ *
143â†’ * @see Instant.toLocalDateTime
144â†’ * @see TimeZone.offsetAt
145â†’ */
146â†’public fun Instant.offsetIn(timeZone: TimeZone): UtcOffset =
147â†’        timeZone.offsetAt(this)
148â†’
149â†’/**
150â†’ * Returns an instant that corresponds to this civil date/time value in the specified [timeZone].
151â†’ *
152â†’ * Note that the conversion is not always unambiguous. There can be the following possible situations:
153â†’ * - There's only one instant that has this date/time value in the [timeZone]. In this case
154â†’ * the conversion is unambiguous.
155â†’ * - There's no instant that has this date/time value in the [timeZone]. Such situation appears when
156â†’ * the time zone experiences a transition from a lesser to a greater offset. In this case the conversion is performed with
157â†’ * the lesser offset.
158â†’ * - There are two possible instants that can have this date/time components in the [timeZone]. In this case the earlier
159â†’ * instant is returned.
160â†’ *
161â†’ * @see Instant.toLocalDateTime
162â†’ */
163â†’public expect fun LocalDateTime.toInstant(timeZone: TimeZone): Instant
164â†’
165â†’/**
166â†’ * Returns an instant that corresponds to this civil date/time value that happens at the specified [UTC offset][offset].
167â†’ *
168â†’ * @see Instant.toLocalDateTime
169â†’ */
170â†’public expect fun LocalDateTime.toInstant(offset: UtcOffset): Instant
171â†’
172â†’/**
173â†’ * Returns an instant that corresponds to the start of this date in the specified [timeZone].
174â†’ *
175â†’ * Note that it's not equivalent to `atTime(0, 0).toInstant(timeZone)`
176â†’ * because a day does not always start at the fixed time 0:00:00.
177â†’ * For example, if due do daylight saving time, clocks were shifted from 23:30
178â†’ * of one day directly to 00:30 of the next day, skipping the midnight, then
179â†’ * `atStartOfDayIn` would return the `Instant` corresponding to 00:30, whereas
180â†’ * `atTime(0, 0).toInstant(timeZone)` would return the `Instant` corresponding
181â†’ * to 01:00.
182â†’ */
183â†’public expect fun LocalDate.atStartOfDayIn(timeZone: TimeZone): Instant
184â†’

1â†’/*
 2â†’ * Copyright 2019-2020 JetBrains s.r.o.
 3â†’ * Use of this source code is governed by the Apache 2.0 License that can be found in the LICENSE.txt file.
 4â†’ */
 5â†’
 6â†’package kotlinx.datetime
 7â†’
 8â†’import kotlin.time.*
 9â†’
10â†’/**
11â†’ * A source of [Instant] values.
12â†’ *
13â†’ * See [Clock.System][Clock.System] for the clock instance that queries the operating system.
14â†’ */
15â†’public interface Clock {
16â†’    /**
17â†’     * Returns the [Instant] corresponding to the current time, according to this clock.
18â†’     */
19â†’    public fun now(): Instant
20â†’
21â†’    /**
22â†’     * The [Clock] instance that queries the operating system as its source of knowledge of time.
23â†’     */
24â†’    public object System : Clock {
25â†’        override fun now(): Instant = @Suppress("DEPRECATION_ERROR") Instant.now()
26â†’    }
27â†’
28â†’    public companion object {
29â†’
30â†’    }
31â†’}
32â†’
33â†’/**
34â†’ * Returns the current date at the given [time zone][timeZone], according to [this Clock][this].
35â†’ */
36â†’public fun Clock.todayIn(timeZone: TimeZone): LocalDate =
37â†’    now().toLocalDateTime(timeZone).date
38â†’
39â†’/**
40â†’ * Returns a [TimeSource] that uses this [Clock] to mark a time instant and to find the amount of time elapsed since that mark.
41â†’ */
42â†’@ExperimentalTime
43â†’public fun Clock.asTimeSource(): TimeSource.WithComparableMarks = object : TimeSource.WithComparableMarks {
44â†’    override fun markNow(): ComparableTimeMark = InstantTimeMark(now(), this@asTimeSource)
45â†’}
46â†’
47â†’@ExperimentalTime
48â†’private class InstantTimeMark(private val instant: Instant, private val clock: Clock) : ComparableTimeMark {
49â†’    override fun elapsedNow(): Duration = saturatingDiff(clock.now(), instant)
50â†’
51â†’    override fun plus(duration: Duration): ComparableTimeMark = InstantTimeMark(instant.saturatingAdd(duration), clock)
52â†’    override fun minus(duration: Duration): ComparableTimeMark = InstantTimeMark(instant.saturatingAdd(-duration), clock)
53â†’
54â†’    override fun minus(other: ComparableTimeMark): Duration {
55â†’        if (other !is InstantTimeMark || other.clock != this.clock) {
56â†’            throw IllegalArgumentException("Subtracting or comparing time marks from different time sources is not possible: $this and $other")
57â†’        }
58â†’        return saturatingDiff(this.instant, other.instant)
59â†’    }
60â†’
61â†’    override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean {
62â†’        return other is InstantTimeMark && this.clock == other.clock && this.instant == other.instant
63â†’    }
64â†’
65â†’    override fun hashCode(): Int = instant.hashCode()
66â†’
67â†’    override fun toString(): String = "InstantTimeMark($instant, $clock)"
68â†’
69â†’    private fun Instant.isSaturated() = this == Instant.MAX || this == Instant.MIN
70â†’    private fun Instant.saturatingAdd(duration: Duration): Instant {
71â†’        if (isSaturated()) {
72â†’            if (duration.isInfinite() && duration.isPositive() != this.isDistantFuture) {
73â†’                throw IllegalArgumentException("Summing infinities of different signs")
74â†’            }
75â†’            return this
76â†’        }
77â†’        return this + duration
78â†’    }
79â†’    private fun saturatingDiff(instant1: Instant, instant2: Instant): Duration = when {
80â†’        instant1 == instant2 ->
81â†’            Duration.ZERO
82â†’        instant1.isSaturated() || instant2.isSaturated() ->
83â†’            (instant1 - instant2) * Double.POSITIVE_INFINITY
84â†’        else ->
85â†’            instant1 - instant2
86â†’    }
87â†’}
88â†’
89â†’@Deprecated("Use Clock.todayIn instead", ReplaceWith("this.todayIn(timeZone)"), DeprecationLevel.WARNING)
90â†’public fun Clock.todayAt(timeZone: TimeZone): LocalDate = todayIn(timeZone)
91â†’

1â†’/*
  2â†’ * Copyright 2019-2022 JetBrains s.r.o. and contributors.
  3â†’ * Use of this source code is governed by the Apache 2.0 License that can be found in the LICENSE.txt file.

  5â†’
  6â†’package kotlinx.datetime
  7â†’
  8â†’import kotlinx.datetime.LocalDate.Companion.parse
  9â†’import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
 10â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.LocalDateTimeIso8601Serializer
 11â†’import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 12â†’
 13â†’/**
 14â†’ * The representation of a specific civil date and time without a reference to a particular time zone.
 15â†’ *
 16â†’ * This class does not describe specific *moments in time*, which are represented as [Instant] values.
 17â†’ * Instead, its instances can be thought of as clock readings, something that an observer in a particular time zone
 18â†’ * could witness.
 19â†’ * For example, `2020-08-30T18:43` is not a *moment in time*, since someone in Berlin and someone in Tokyo would witness
 20â†’ * this on their clocks at different times.
 21â†’ *
 22â†’ * The main purpose of this class is to provide human-readable representations of [Instant] values, or to transfer them
 23â†’ * as data.
 24â†’ *
 25â†’ * The arithmetic on [LocalDateTime] values is not provided, since without accounting for the time zone transitions it may give misleading results.
 26â†’ */
 27â†’@Serializable(with = LocalDateTimeIso8601Serializer::class)
 28â†’public expect class LocalDateTime : Comparable<LocalDateTime> {
 29â†’    public companion object {
 30â†’
 31â†’        /**
 32â†’         * A shortcut for calling [DateTimeFormat.parse].
 33â†’         *
 34â†’         * Parses a string that represents a date/time value including date and time components
 35â†’         * but without any time zone component and returns the parsed [LocalDateTime] value.
 36â†’         *
 37â†’         * If [format] is not specified, [Formats.ISO] is used.
 38â†’         *
 39â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [LocalDateTime] are
 40â†’         * exceeded.
 41â†’         */
 42â†’        public fun parse(input: CharSequence, format: DateTimeFormat<LocalDateTime> = getIsoDateTimeFormat()): LocalDateTime
 43â†’
 44â†’        /**
 45â†’         * Creates a new format for parsing and formatting [LocalDateTime] values.
 46â†’         *
 47â†’         * Examples:
 48â†’         * ```
 49â†’         * // `2020-08-30 18:43:13`, using predefined date and time formats
 50â†’         * LocalDateTime.Format { date(LocalDate.Formats.ISO); char(' '); time(LocalTime.Formats.ISO) }
 51â†’         *
 52â†’         * // `08/30 18:43:13`, using a custom format:
 53â†’         * LocalDateTime.Format {
 54â†’         *   monthNumber(); char('/'); dayOfMonth()
 55â†’         *   char(' ')
 56â†’         *   hour(); char(':'); minute()
 57â†’         *   optional { char(':'); second() }
 58â†’         * }
 59â†’         * ```
 60â†’         *
 61â†’         * Only parsing and formatting of well-formed values is supported. If the input does not fit the boundaries
 62â†’         * (for example, [dayOfMonth] is 31 for February), consider using [DateTimeComponents.Format] instead.
 63â†’         *
 64â†’         * There is a collection of predefined formats in [LocalDateTime.Formats].
 65â†’         *
 66â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if parsing using this format is ambiguous.
 67â†’         */
 68â†’        @Suppress("FunctionName")
 69â†’        public fun Format(builder: DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDateTime.() -> Unit): DateTimeFormat<LocalDateTime>
 70â†’
 71â†’        internal val MIN: LocalDateTime
 72â†’        internal val MAX: LocalDateTime
 73â†’    }
 74â†’
 75â†’    /**
 76â†’     * A collection of predefined formats for parsing and formatting [LocalDateTime] values.
 77â†’     *
 78â†’     * [LocalDateTime.Formats.ISO] is a popular predefined format.
 79â†’     *
 80â†’     * If predefined formats are not sufficient, use [LocalDateTime.Format] to create a custom
 81â†’     * [kotlinx.datetime.format.DateTimeFormat] for [LocalDateTime] values.
 82â†’     */
 83â†’    public object Formats {
 84â†’        /**
 85â†’         * ISO 8601 extended format.
 86â†’         *
 87â†’         * Examples of date/time in ISO 8601 format:
 88â†’         * - `2020-08-30T18:43`
 89â†’         * - `+12020-08-30T18:43:00`
 90â†’         * - `0000-08-30T18:43:00.5`
 91â†’         * - `-0001-08-30T18:43:00.123456789`
 92â†’         *
 93â†’         * When formatting, seconds are always included, even if they are zero.
 94â†’         * Fractional parts of the second are included if non-zero.
 95â†’         *
 96â†’         * Guaranteed to parse all strings that [LocalDateTime.toString] produces.
 97â†’         */
 98â†’        public val ISO: DateTimeFormat<LocalDateTime>
 99â†’    }
100â†’
101â†’    /**
102â†’     * Constructs a [LocalDateTime] instance from the given date and time components.
103â†’     *
104â†’     * The components [monthNumber] and [dayOfMonth] are 1-based.
105â†’     *
106â†’     * The supported ranges of components:
107â†’     * - [year] the range is platform dependent, but at least is enough to represent dates of all instants between
108â†’     *          [Instant.DISTANT_PAST] and [Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE]
109â†’     * - [monthNumber] `1..12`
110â†’     * - [dayOfMonth] `1..31`, the upper bound can be less, depending on the month
111â†’     * - [hour] `0..23`
112â†’     * - [minute] `0..59`
113â†’     * - [second] `0..59`
114â†’     * - [nanosecond] `0..999_999_999`
115â†’     *
116â†’     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if any parameter is out of range, or if [dayOfMonth] is invalid for the given [monthNumber] and
117â†’     * [year].
118â†’     */
119â†’    public constructor(
120â†’        year: Int,
121â†’        monthNumber: Int,
122â†’        dayOfMonth: Int,
123â†’        hour: Int,
124â†’        minute: Int,
125â†’        second: Int = 0,
126â†’        nanosecond: Int = 0
127â†’    )
128â†’
129â†’    /**
130â†’     * Constructs a [LocalDateTime] instance from the given date and time components.
131â†’     *
132â†’     * The supported ranges of components:
133â†’     * - [year] the range is platform dependent, but at least is enough to represent dates of all instants between
134â†’     *          [Instant.DISTANT_PAST] and [Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE]
135â†’     * - [month] all values of the [Month] enum
136â†’     * - [dayOfMonth] `1..31`, the upper bound can be less, depending on the month
137â†’     * - [hour] `0..23`
138â†’     * - [minute] `0..59`
139â†’     * - [second] `0..59`
140â†’     * - [nanosecond] `0..999_999_999`
141â†’     *
142â†’     * @throws IllegalArgumentException if any parameter is out of range, or if [dayOfMonth] is invalid for the given [month] and
143â†’     * [year].
144â†’     */
145â†’    public constructor(
146â†’        year: Int,
147â†’        month: Month,
148â†’        dayOfMonth: Int,
149â†’        hour: Int,
150â†’        minute: Int,
151â†’        second: Int = 0,
152â†’        nanosecond: Int = 0
153â†’    )
154â†’
155â†’    /**
156â†’     * Constructs a [LocalDateTime] instance by combining the given [date] and [time] parts.
157â†’     */
158â†’    public constructor(date: LocalDate, time: LocalTime)
159â†’
160â†’    /** Returns the year component of the date. */

162â†’

164â†’    public val monthNumber: Int
165â†’

177â†’

180â†’

183â†’

186â†’

192â†’

1â†’/*

 4â†’ */
 5â†’
 6â†’package kotlinx.datetime
 7â†’
 8â†’/**
 9â†’ * The enumeration class representing the days of the week.
10â†’ */
11â†’public expect enum class DayOfWeek {
12â†’    MONDAY,
13â†’    TUESDAY,
14â†’    WEDNESDAY,
15â†’    THURSDAY,
16â†’    FRIDAY,
17â†’    SATURDAY,
18â†’    SUNDAY;
19â†’}
20â†’
21â†’/**
22â†’ * The ISO-8601 number of the given day of the week. Monday is 1, Sunday is 7.
23â†’ */
24â†’public val DayOfWeek.isoDayNumber: Int get() = ordinal + 1
25â†’
26â†’/**
27â†’ * Returns the [DayOfWeek] instance for the given ISO-8601 week day number. Monday is 1, Sunday is 7.
28â†’ */
29â†’public fun DayOfWeek(isoDayNumber: Int): DayOfWeek {
30â†’    require(isoDayNumber in 1..7) { "Expected ISO day-of-week number in 1..7, got $isoDayNumber" }
31â†’    return DayOfWeek.entries[isoDayNumber - 1]
32â†’}
33â†’

  6â†’package kotlinx.datetime
  7â†’
  8â†’import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
  9â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.UtcOffsetSerializer
 10â†’import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 11â†’
 12â†’/**
 13â†’ * An offset from UTC.
 14â†’ *
 15â†’ * Examples of these values:
 16â†’ * - `Z`, an offset of zero;
 17â†’ * - `+05`, plus five hours;
 18â†’ * - `-02`, minus two hours;
 19â†’ * - `+03:30`, plus three hours and thirty minutes;
 20â†’ * - `+01:23:45`, plus one hour, 23 minutes, and 45 seconds.
 21â†’ */
 22â†’@Serializable(with = UtcOffsetSerializer::class)
 23â†’public expect class UtcOffset {
 24â†’    /**
 25â†’     * The number of seconds from UTC.
 26â†’     *
 27â†’     * The larger the value, the earlier some specific civil date/time happens with the offset.
 28â†’     */
 29â†’    public val totalSeconds: Int
 30â†’
 31â†’    // TODO: Declare and document toString/equals/hashCode
 32â†’
 33â†’    public companion object {
 34â†’        /**
 35â†’         * The zero offset from UTC, `Z`.
 36â†’         */
 37â†’        public val ZERO: UtcOffset
 38â†’
 39â†’        /**
 40â†’         * A shortcut for calling [DateTimeFormat.parse].
 41â†’         *
 42â†’         * Parses a string that represents a UTC offset and returns the parsed [UtcOffset] value.
 43â†’         *
 44â†’         * If [format] is not specified, [Formats.ISO] is used.
 45â†’         *
 46â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [UtcOffset] are
 47â†’         * exceeded.
 48â†’         */
 49â†’        public fun parse(input: CharSequence, format: DateTimeFormat<UtcOffset> = getIsoUtcOffestFormat()): UtcOffset
 50â†’
 51â†’        /**
 52â†’         * Creates a new format for parsing and formatting [UtcOffset] values.
 53â†’         *
 54â†’         * Example:
 55â†’         * ```
 56â†’         * // `GMT` on zero, `+4:30:15`, using a custom format:
 57â†’         * UtcOffset.Format {
 58â†’         *   optional("GMT") {
 59â†’         *     offsetHours(Padding.NONE); char(':'); offsetMinutesOfHour()
 60â†’         *     optional { char(':'); offsetSecondsOfMinute() }
 61â†’         *   }
 62â†’         * }
 63â†’         * ```
 64â†’         *
 65â†’         * Since [UtcOffset] values are rarely formatted and parsed on their own,
 66â†’         * instances of [DateTimeFormat] obtained here will likely need to be passed to
 67â†’         * [DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithUtcOffset.offset] in a format builder for a larger data structure.
 68â†’         *
 69â†’         * There is a collection of predefined formats in [UtcOffset.Formats].
 70â†’         *
 71â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if parsing using this format is ambiguous.
 72â†’         */
 73â†’        @Suppress("FunctionName")
 74â†’        public fun Format(block: DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithUtcOffset.() -> Unit): DateTimeFormat<UtcOffset>
 75â†’    }
 76â†’
 77â†’    /**
 78â†’     * A collection of predefined formats for parsing and formatting [UtcOffset] values.
 79â†’     *
 80â†’     * See [UtcOffset.Formats.ISO], [UtcOffset.Formats.ISO_BASIC], and [UtcOffset.Formats.FOUR_DIGITS]
 81â†’     * for popular predefined formats.
 82â†’     *
 83â†’     * If predefined formats are not sufficient, use [UtcOffset.Format] to create a custom
 84â†’     * [kotlinx.datetime.format.DateTimeFormat] for [UtcOffset] values.
 85â†’     */
 86â†’    public object Formats {
 87â†’        /**
 88â†’         * ISO 8601 extended format, which is the format used by [UtcOffset.parse] and [UtcOffset.toString].
 89â†’         *
 90â†’         * An extension of the ISO 8601 is that this format allows parsing and formatting seconds.
 91â†’         *
 92â†’         * When formatting, seconds are omitted if they are zero. If the whole offset is zero, the letter `Z` is output.
 93â†’         *
 94â†’         * Examples of UTC offsets in ISO 8601 format:
 95â†’         * - `Z` or `+00:00`, an offset of zero;
 96â†’         * - `+05:00`, five hours;
 97â†’         * - `-02:00`, minus two hours;
 98â†’         * - `-17:16`
 99â†’         * - `+10:36:30`
100â†’         */
101â†’        public val ISO: DateTimeFormat<UtcOffset>
102â†’
103â†’        /**
104â†’         * ISO 8601 basic format.
105â†’         *
106â†’         * An extension of the ISO 8601 is that this format allows parsing and formatting seconds.
107â†’         *
108â†’         * When formatting, seconds are omitted if they are zero. If the whole offset is zero, the letter `Z` is output.
109â†’         *
110â†’         * Examples of UTC offsets in ISO 8601 basic format:
111â†’         * - `Z`
112â†’         * - `+05`
113â†’         * - `-1716`
114â†’         * - `+103630`
115â†’         *
116â†’         * @see UtcOffset.Formats.FOUR_DIGITS
117â†’         */
118â†’        public val ISO_BASIC: DateTimeFormat<UtcOffset>
119â†’
120â†’        /**
121â†’         * A subset of the ISO 8601 basic format that always outputs and parses exactly a numeric sign and four digits:
122â†’         * two digits for the hour and two digits for the minute. If the offset has a non-zero number of seconds,
123â†’         * they are truncated.
124â†’         *
125â†’         * Examples of UTC offsets in this format:
126â†’         * - `+0000`
127â†’         * - `+0500`
128â†’         * - `-1716`
129â†’         * - `+1036`
130â†’         *
131â†’         * @see UtcOffset.Formats.ISO_BASIC
132â†’         */
133â†’        public val FOUR_DIGITS: DateTimeFormat<UtcOffset>
134â†’    }
135â†’
136â†’    /**
137â†’     * Converts this UTC offset to the extended ISO-8601 string representation.
138â†’     *
139â†’     * @see Formats.ISO for the format details.
140â†’     * @see parse for the dual operation: obtaining [UtcOffset] from a string.
141â†’     * @see UtcOffset.format for formatting using a custom format.
142â†’     */
143â†’    public override fun toString(): String
144â†’}
145â†’
146â†’/**
147â†’ * Formats this value using the given [format].
148â†’ * Equivalent to calling [DateTimeFormat.format] on [format] with `this`.
149â†’ */
150â†’public fun UtcOffset.format(format: DateTimeFormat<UtcOffset>): String = format.format(this)
151â†’
152â†’/**
153â†’ * Constructs a [UtcOffset] from hours, minutes, and seconds components.
154â†’ *
155â†’ * All components must have the same sign.
156â†’ *
157â†’ * The bounds are checked: it is invalid to pass something other than `Â±[0; 59]` as the number of seconds or minutes.
158â†’ * For example, `UtcOffset(hours = 3, minutes = 61)` is invalid.
159â†’ *
160â†’ * However, the non-null component of the highest order can exceed these bounds,
161â†’ * for example, `UtcOffset(minutes = 241)` is valid.
162â†’ *
163â†’ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if a component exceeds its bounds when a higher order component is specified.
164â†’ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if components have different signs.
165â†’ * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the resulting `UtcOffset` value is outside of range `Â±18:00`.
166â†’ */
167â†’public expect fun UtcOffset(hours: Int? = null, minutes: Int? = null, seconds: Int? = null): UtcOffset
168â†’
169â†’@Deprecated("Use UtcOffset.ZERO instead", ReplaceWith("UtcOffset.ZERO"), DeprecationLevel.ERROR)
170â†’public fun UtcOffset(): UtcOffset = UtcOffset.ZERO
171â†’
172â†’/**
173â†’ * Returns the fixed-offset time zone with the given UTC offset.
174â†’ */
175â†’public fun UtcOffset.asTimeZone(): FixedOffsetTimeZone = FixedOffsetTimeZone(this)
176â†’
177â†’// workaround for https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/KT-65484
178â†’internal fun getIsoUtcOffestFormat() = UtcOffset.Formats.ISO
179â†’

 10â†’import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 11â†’
 12â†’/**
 13â†’ * The date part of [LocalDateTime].
 14â†’ *
 15â†’ * This class represents dates without a reference to a particular time zone.
 16â†’ * As such, these objects may denote different spans of time in different time zones: for someone in Berlin,
 17â†’ * `2020-08-30` started and ended at different moments from those for someone in Tokyo.
 18â†’ *
 19â†’ * The arithmetic on [LocalDate] values is defined independently of the time zone (so `2020-08-30` plus one day
 20â†’ * is `2020-08-31` everywhere): see various [LocalDate.plus] and [LocalDate.minus] functions, as well
 21â†’ * as [LocalDate.periodUntil] and various other [*until][LocalDate.daysUntil] functions.
 22â†’ */
 23â†’@Serializable(with = LocalDateIso8601Serializer::class)
 24â†’public expect class LocalDate : Comparable<LocalDate> {
 25â†’    public companion object {
 26â†’        /**
 27â†’         * A shortcut for calling [DateTimeFormat.parse].
 28â†’         *
 29â†’         * Parses a string that represents a date and returns the parsed [LocalDate] value.
 30â†’         *
 31â†’         * If [format] is not specified, [Formats.ISO] is used.
 32â†’         *
 33â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [LocalDate] are exceeded.
 34â†’         *
 35â†’         * @see LocalDate.toString for formatting using the default format.
 36â†’         * @see LocalDate.format for formatting using a custom format.
 37â†’         */
 38â†’        public fun parse(input: CharSequence, format: DateTimeFormat<LocalDate> = getIsoDateFormat()): LocalDate
 39â†’
 40â†’        /**
 41â†’         * Returns a [LocalDate] that is [epochDays] number of days from the epoch day `1970-01-01`.
 42â†’         *
 43â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the result exceeds the platform-specific boundaries of [LocalDate].
 44â†’         *
 45â†’         * @see LocalDate.toEpochDays
 46â†’         */
 47â†’        public fun fromEpochDays(epochDays: Int): LocalDate
 48â†’
 49â†’        /**
 50â†’         * Creates a new format for parsing and formatting [LocalDate] values.
 51â†’         *
 52â†’         * Example:
 53â†’         * ```
 54â†’         * // 2020 Jan 05
 55â†’         * LocalDate.Format {
 56â†’         *   year()
 57â†’         *   char(' ')
 58â†’         *   monthName(MonthNames.ENGLISH_ABBREVIATED)
 59â†’         *   char(' ')
 60â†’         *   dayOfMonth()
 61â†’         * }
 62â†’         * ```
 63â†’         *
 64â†’         * Only parsing and formatting of well-formed values is supported. If the input does not fit the boundaries
 65â†’         * (for example, [dayOfMonth] is 31 for February), consider using [DateTimeComponents.Format] instead.
 66â†’         *
 67â†’         * There is a collection of predefined formats in [LocalDate.Formats].
 68â†’         *
 69â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if parsing using this format is ambiguous.
 70â†’         */
 71â†’        @Suppress("FunctionName")
 72â†’        public fun Format(block: DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithDate.() -> Unit): DateTimeFormat<LocalDate>
 73â†’
 74â†’        internal val MIN: LocalDate
 75â†’        internal val MAX: LocalDate
 76â†’    }
 77â†’
 78â†’    /**
 79â†’     * A collection of predefined formats for parsing and formatting [LocalDate] values.
 80â†’     *
 81â†’     * See [LocalDate.Formats.ISO] and [LocalDate.Formats.ISO_BASIC] for popular predefined formats.
 82â†’     * [LocalDate.parse] and [LocalDate.toString] can be used as convenient shortcuts for the
 83â†’     * [LocalDate.Formats.ISO] format.
 84â†’     *
 85â†’     * If predefined formats are not sufficient, use [LocalDate.Format] to create a custom
 86â†’     * [kotlinx.datetime.format.DateTimeFormat] for [LocalDate] values.
 87â†’     */
 88â†’    public object Formats {
 89â†’        /**
 90â†’         * ISO 8601 extended format, which is the format used by [LocalDate.toString] and [LocalDate.parse].
 91â†’         *
 92â†’         * Examples of dates in ISO 8601 format:
 93â†’         * - `2020-08-30`
 94â†’         * - `+12020-08-30`
 95â†’         * - `0000-08-30`
 96â†’         * - `-0001-08-30`
 97â†’         */
 98â†’        public val ISO: DateTimeFormat<LocalDate>
 99â†’
100â†’        /**
101â†’         * ISO 8601 basic format.
102â†’         *
103â†’         * Examples of dates in ISO 8601 basic format:
104â†’         * - `20200830`
105â†’         * - `+120200830`
106â†’         * - `00000830`
107â†’         * - `-00010830`
108â†’         */
109â†’        public val ISO_BASIC: DateTimeFormat<LocalDate>
110â†’    }
111â†’
112â†’    /**
113â†’     * Constructs a [LocalDate] instance from the given date components.
114â†’     *
115â†’     * The components [monthNumber] and [dayOfMonth] are 1-based.
116â†’     *
117â†’     * The supported ranges of components:
118â†’     * - [year] the range is platform dependent, but at least is enough to represent dates of all instants between
119â†’     *          [Instant.DISTANT_PAST] and [Instant.DISTANT_FUTURE]
120â†’     * - [monthNumber] `1..12`

  6â†’package kotlinx.datetime
  7â†’
  8â†’import kotlinx.datetime.LocalDate.Companion.parse
  9â†’import kotlinx.datetime.format.*
 10â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.LocalTimeIso8601Serializer
 11â†’import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 12â†’
 13â†’
 14â†’/**
 15â†’ * The time part of [LocalDateTime].
 16â†’ *
 17â†’ * This class represents time-of-day without a referencing a specific date.
 18â†’ * To reconstruct a full [LocalDateTime], representing civil date and time, [LocalTime] needs to be
 19â†’ * combined with [LocalDate] via [LocalDate.atTime] or [LocalTime.atDate].
 20â†’ *
 21â†’ * Also, [LocalTime] does not reference a particular time zone.
 22â†’ * Therefore, even on the same date, [LocalTime] denotes different moments of time.
 23â†’ * For example, `18:43` happens at different moments in Berlin and in Tokyo.
 24â†’ *
 25â†’ * The arithmetic on [LocalTime] values is not provided, since without accounting for the time zone
 26â†’ * transitions it may give misleading results.
 27â†’ */
 28â†’@Serializable(LocalTimeIso8601Serializer::class)
 29â†’public expect class LocalTime : Comparable<LocalTime> {
 30â†’    public companion object {
 31â†’
 32â†’        /**
 33â†’         * A shortcut for calling [DateTimeFormat.parse].
 34â†’         *
 35â†’         * Parses a string that represents time-of-day and returns the parsed [LocalTime] value.
 36â†’         *
 37â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if the text cannot be parsed or the boundaries of [LocalTime] are
 38â†’         * exceeded.
 39â†’         *
 40â†’         * @see LocalTime.toString for formatting using the default format.
 41â†’         * @see LocalTime.format for formatting using a custom format.
 42â†’         */
 43â†’        public fun parse(input: CharSequence, format: DateTimeFormat<LocalTime> = getIsoTimeFormat()): LocalTime
 44â†’
 45â†’        /**
 46â†’         * Constructs a [LocalTime] that represents the specified number of seconds since the start of a calendar day.
 47â†’         * The fractional parts of the second will be zero.
 48â†’         *
 49â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if [secondOfDay] is outside the `0 until 86400` range,
 50â†’         * with 86400 being the number of seconds in a calendar day.
 51â†’         *
 52â†’         * @see LocalTime.toSecondOfDay
 53â†’         * @see LocalTime.fromMillisecondOfDay
 54â†’         * @see LocalTime.fromNanosecondOfDay
 55â†’         */
 56â†’        public fun fromSecondOfDay(secondOfDay: Int): LocalTime
 57â†’
 58â†’        /**
 59â†’         * Constructs a [LocalTime] that represents the specified number of milliseconds since the start of
 60â†’         * a calendar day.
 61â†’         * The sub-millisecond parts of the `LocalTime` will be zero.
 62â†’         *
 63â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if [millisecondOfDay] is outside the `0 until 86400 * 1_000` range,
 64â†’         * with 86400 being the number of seconds in a calendar day.
 65â†’         *
 66â†’         * @see LocalTime.fromSecondOfDay
 67â†’         * @see LocalTime.toMillisecondOfDay
 68â†’         * @see LocalTime.fromNanosecondOfDay
 69â†’         */
 70â†’        public fun fromMillisecondOfDay(millisecondOfDay: Int): LocalTime
 71â†’
 72â†’        /**
 73â†’         * Constructs a [LocalTime] that represents the specified number of nanoseconds since the start of
 74â†’         * a calendar day.
 75â†’         *
 76â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if [nanosecondOfDay] is outside the `0 until 86400 * 1_000_000_000` range,
 77â†’         * with 86400 being the number of seconds in a calendar day.
 78â†’         *
 79â†’         * @see LocalTime.fromSecondOfDay
 80â†’         * @see LocalTime.fromMillisecondOfDay
 81â†’         * @see LocalTime.toNanosecondOfDay
 82â†’         */
 83â†’        public fun fromNanosecondOfDay(nanosecondOfDay: Long): LocalTime
 84â†’
 85â†’        /**
 86â†’         * Creates a new format for parsing and formatting [LocalTime] values.
 87â†’         *
 88â†’         * Example:
 89â†’         * ```
 90â†’         * LocalTime.Format {
 91â†’         *   hour(); char(':'); minute(); char(':'); second()
 92â†’         *   optional { char('.'); secondFraction() }
 93â†’         * }
 94â†’         * ```
 95â†’         *
 96â†’         * Only parsing and formatting of well-formed values is supported. If the input does not fit the boundaries
 97â†’         * (for example, [second] is 60), consider using [DateTimeComponents.Format] instead.
 98â†’         *
 99â†’         * There is a collection of predefined formats in [LocalTime.Formats].
100â†’         *
101â†’         * @throws IllegalArgumentException if parsing using this format is ambiguous.
102â†’         */
103â†’        @Suppress("FunctionName")
104â†’        public fun Format(builder: DateTimeFormatBuilder.WithTime.() -> Unit): DateTimeFormat<LocalTime>
105â†’
106â†’        internal val MIN: LocalTime
107â†’        internal val MAX: LocalTime
108â†’    }
109â†’
110â†’    /**
111â†’     * A collection of predefined formats for parsing and formatting [LocalDateTime] values.
112â†’     *
113â†’     * [LocalTime.Formats.ISO] is a popular predefined format.
114â†’     *
115â†’     * If predefined formats are not sufficient, use [LocalTime.Format] to create a custom
116â†’     * [kotlinx.datetime.format.DateTimeFormat] for [LocalTime] values.
117â†’     */
118â†’    public object Formats {
119â†’        /**
120â†’         * ISO 8601 extended format.
121â†’         *
122â†’         * Examples: `12:34`, `12:34:56`, `12:34:56.789`, `12:34:56.1234`.
123â†’         *
124â†’         * When formatting, seconds are always included, even if they are zero.
125â†’         * Fractional parts of the second are included if non-zero.
126â†’         *
127â†’         * Guaranteed to parse all strings that [LocalTime.toString] produces.
128â†’         */
129â†’        public val ISO: DateTimeFormat<LocalTime>
130â†’    }
131â†’
132â†’    /**
133â†’     * Constructs a [LocalTime] instance from the given time components.
134â†’     *
135â†’     * The supported ranges of components:
136â†’     * - [hour] `0..23`
137â†’     * - [minute] `0..59`
138â†’     * - [second] `0..59`
139â†’     * - [nanosecond] `0..999_999_999`
140â†’     *

1â†’/*
  2â†’ * Copyright 2019-2020 JetBrains s.r.o.
  3â†’ * Use of this source code is governed by the Apache 2.0 License that can be found in the LICENSE.txt file.
  4â†’ */
  5â†’
  6â†’package kotlinx.datetime
  7â†’
  8â†’import kotlinx.datetime.internal.safeMultiply
  9â†’import kotlinx.datetime.serializers.*
 10â†’import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
 11â†’import kotlin.time.*
 12â†’import kotlin.time.Duration.Companion.nanoseconds
 13â†’
 14â†’/**
 15â†’ * A unit for measuring time.
 16â†’ *
 17â†’ * See the predefined constants for time units, like [DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND], [DateTimeUnit.DAY],
 18â†’ * [DateTimeUnit.MONTH], and others.
 19â†’ *
 20â†’ * Two ways are provided to create custom [DateTimeUnit] instances:
 21â†’ * - By multiplying an existing unit on the right by an integer scalar: for example, `DateTimeUnit.NANOSECOND * 10`.
 22â†’ * - By constructing an instance manually with [TimeBased], [DayBased], or [MonthBased]: for example,
 23â†’ *   `TimeBased(nanoseconds = 10)`.
 24â†’ *
 25â†’ * Note that a calendar day is not considered identical to 24 hours. See [DateTimeUnit.DayBased] for a discussion.
 26â†’ */
 27â†’@Serializable(with = DateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
 28â†’public sealed class DateTimeUnit {
 29â†’
 30â†’    /** Produces a date-time unit that is a multiple of this unit times the specified integer [scalar] value. */
 31â†’    public abstract operator fun times(scalar: Int): DateTimeUnit
 32â†’
 33â†’    /**
 34â†’     * A date-time unit that has the precise time duration.
 35â†’     *
 36â†’     * Such units are independent of the time zone.
 37â†’     * Any such unit can be represented as some fixed number of nanoseconds.
 38â†’     */
 39â†’    @Serializable(with = TimeBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
 40â†’    public class TimeBased(
 41â†’        /**
 42â†’         * The length of this unit in nanoseconds.
 43â†’         */
 44â†’        public val nanoseconds: Long
 45â†’    ) : DateTimeUnit() {
 46â†’        private val unitName: String
 47â†’        private val unitScale: Long
 48â†’
 49â†’        init {
 50â†’            require(nanoseconds > 0) { "Unit duration must be positive, but was $nanoseconds ns." }
 51â†’            // find a concise string representation for the unit with this duration
 52â†’            when {
 53â†’                nanoseconds % 3600_000_000_000 == 0L -> {
 54â†’                    unitName = "HOUR"
 55â†’                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 3600_000_000_000
 56â†’                }
 57â†’                nanoseconds % 60_000_000_000 == 0L -> {
 58â†’                    unitName = "MINUTE"
 59â†’                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 60_000_000_000
 60â†’                }
 61â†’                nanoseconds % 1_000_000_000 == 0L -> {
 62â†’                    unitName = "SECOND"
 63â†’                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 1_000_000_000
 64â†’                }
 65â†’                nanoseconds % 1_000_000 == 0L -> {
 66â†’                    unitName = "MILLISECOND"
 67â†’                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 1_000_000
 68â†’                }
 69â†’                nanoseconds % 1_000 == 0L -> {
 70â†’                    unitName = "MICROSECOND"
 71â†’                    unitScale = nanoseconds / 1_000
 72â†’                }
 73â†’                else -> {
 74â†’                    unitName = "NANOSECOND"
 75â†’                    unitScale = nanoseconds
 76â†’                }
 77â†’            }
 78â†’        }
 79â†’
 80â†’        override fun times(scalar: Int): TimeBased = TimeBased(safeMultiply(nanoseconds, scalar.toLong()))
 81â†’
 82â†’        /**
 83â†’         * The length of this unit as a [Duration].
 84â†’         */
 85â†’        public val duration: Duration
 86â†’            get() = nanoseconds.nanoseconds
 87â†’
 88â†’        override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
 89â†’            this === other || (other is TimeBased && this.nanoseconds == other.nanoseconds)
 90â†’
 91â†’        override fun hashCode(): Int = nanoseconds.toInt() xor (nanoseconds shr Int.SIZE_BITS).toInt()
 92â†’
 93â†’        override fun toString(): String = formatToString(unitScale, unitName)
 94â†’    }
 95â†’
 96â†’    /**
 97â†’     * A date-time unit equal to some number of days or months.
 98â†’     *
 99â†’     * Operations involving `DateBased` units are performed on dates. The same operations on [Instants][Instant]
100â†’     * require a [TimeZone] to find the corresponding [LocalDateTimes][LocalDateTime] first to perform
101â†’     * the operation with the date component of these `LocalDateTime` values.
102â†’     */
103â†’    @Serializable(with = DateBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
104â†’    public sealed class DateBased : DateTimeUnit() {
105â†’        @Suppress("TOPLEVEL_TYPEALIASES_ONLY")
106â†’        @Deprecated("Use DateTimeUnit.DayBased", ReplaceWith("DateTimeUnit.DayBased", "kotlinx.datetime.DateTimeUnit"))
107â†’        public typealias DayBased = DateTimeUnit.DayBased
108â†’        @Suppress("TOPLEVEL_TYPEALIASES_ONLY")
109â†’        @Deprecated("Use DateTimeUnit.MonthBased", ReplaceWith("DateTimeUnit.MonthBased", "kotlinx.datetime.DateTimeUnit"))
110â†’        public typealias MonthBased = DateTimeUnit.MonthBased
111â†’    }
112â†’
113â†’    /**
114â†’     * A date-time unit equal to some number of calendar days.
115â†’     *
116â†’     * A calendar day is not considered identical to 24 hours, thus a `DayBased`-unit cannot be expressed as a multiple of some [TimeBased]-unit.
117â†’     *
118â†’     * The reason lies in time zone transitions, because of which some days can be 23 or 25 hours.
119â†’     * For example, we say that exactly a whole day has passed between `2019-10-27T02:59` and `2019-10-28T02:59`
120â†’     * in Berlin, despite the fact that the clocks were turned back one hour, so there are, in fact, 25 hours
121â†’     * between the two date-times.
122â†’     */
123â†’    @Serializable(with = DayBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
124â†’    public class DayBased(
125â†’        /**
126â†’         * The length of this unit in days.
127â†’         */
128â†’        public val days: Int
129â†’    ) : DateBased() {
130â†’        init {
131â†’            require(days > 0) { "Unit duration must be positive, but was $days days." }
132â†’        }
133â†’
134â†’        override fun times(scalar: Int): DateTimeUnit.DayBased = DateTimeUnit.DayBased(safeMultiply(days, scalar))
135â†’
136â†’        override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
137â†’            this === other || (other is DateTimeUnit.DayBased && this.days == other.days)
138â†’
139â†’        override fun hashCode(): Int = days xor 0x10000
140â†’
141â†’        override fun toString(): String = if (days % 7 == 0)
142â†’            formatToString(days / 7, "WEEK")
143â†’        else
144â†’            formatToString(days, "DAY")
145â†’    }
146â†’
147â†’    /**
148â†’     * A date-time unit equal to some number of months.
149â†’     *
150â†’     * Since different months have different number of days, a `MonthBased`-unit cannot be expressed a multiple of some [DayBased]-unit.
151â†’     */
152â†’    @Serializable(with = MonthBasedDateTimeUnitSerializer::class)
153â†’    public class MonthBased(
154â†’        /**
155â†’         * The length of this unit in months.
156â†’         */
157â†’        public val months: Int
158â†’    ) : DateBased() {
159â†’        init {
160â†’            require(months > 0) { "Unit duration must be positive, but was $months months." }
161â†’        }
162â†’
163â†’        override fun times(scalar: Int): DateTimeUnit.MonthBased = DateTimeUnit.MonthBased(safeMultiply(months, scalar))
164â†’
165â†’        override fun equals(other: Any?): Boolean =
166â†’            this === other || (other is DateTimeUnit.MonthBased && this.months == other.months)
167â†’
168â†’        override fun hashCode(): Int = months xor 0x20000
169â†’
170â†’        override fun toString(): String = when {
171â†’            months % 12_00 == 0 -> formatToString(months / 12_00, "CENTURY")
172â†’            months % 12 == 0 -> formatToString(months / 12, "YEAR")
173â†’            months % 3 == 0 -> formatToString(months / 3, "QUARTER")
174â†’            else -> formatToString(months, "MONTH")
175â†’        }
176â†’    }
177â†’
178â†’    protected fun formatToString(value: Int, unit: String): String = if (value == 1) unit else "$value-$unit"
179â†’    protected fun formatToString(value: Long, unit: String): String = if (value == 1L) unit else "$value-$unit"
180â†’
181â†’    public companion object {
182â†’        /**
183â†’         * A nanosecond, which is `1/1_000_000_000` of a second.
184â†’         */
185â†’        public val NANOSECOND: TimeBased = TimeBased(nanoseconds = 1)
186â†’
187â†’        /**
188â†’         * A microsecond, which is `1/1_000_000` of a second, or `1_000` nanoseconds.
189â†’         */
190â†’        public val MICROSECOND: TimeBased = NANOSECOND * 1000
191â†’
192â†’        /**
193â†’         * A millisecond, which is `1/1_000` of a second, or `1_000_000` nanoseconds.
194â†’         */
195â†’        public val MILLISECOND: TimeBased = MICROSECOND * 1000
196â†’
197â†’        /**
198â†’         * A second.
199â†’         */
200â†’        public val SECOND: TimeBased = MILLISECOND * 1000
