{"file_path":"/project/core/common/src/Instant.kt","old_string":"/**
 * A moment in time.
 *
 * A point in time must be uniquely identified, so that it is independent of a time zone.
 * For example, `1970-01-01, 00:00:00` does not represent a moment in time, since this would happen at different times
 * in different time zones: someone in Tokyo would think its already `1970-01-01` several hours earlier than someone in
 * Berlin would. To represent such entities, use [LocalDateTime].
 * In contrast, "the moment the clocks in London first showed 00:00 on Jan 1, 2000" is a specific moment
 * in time, as is "1970-01-01, 00:00:00 UTC+0", and so it can be represented as an [Instant].
 *
 * `Instant` uses the UTC-SLS (smeared leap second) time scale. This time scale doesn't contain instants
 * corresponding to leap seconds, but instead "smears" positive and negative leap seconds among the last 1000 seconds
 * of the day when a leap second happens.
 *
 * Some ways in which [Instant] can be acquired are:
 * - [Clock.now] can be used to query the current moment for the given clock. With [Clock.System], it is the current
 *   moment as the platform sees it.
 * - [Instant.parse] parses an ISO-8601 string.
 * - [Instant.fromEpochMilliseconds] and [Instant.fromEpochSeconds] construct the instant values from the amount of time
 *   since `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` (the Unix epoch).
 */","new_string":"/**
 * A moment in time.
 *
 * [Instant] represents a single point on the time axis, independent of any time zone.
 * For example, `1970-01-01T00:00:00` *without* specifying a time zone is not a moment in time (it is a
 * [LocalDateTime]), because observers in different zones will see that clock reading at different moments.
 * In contrast, `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` or `1970-01-01T00:00:00+00:00` denotes a specific moment and can be represented
 * as an [Instant].
 *
 * ## Time scale
 * `Instant` uses the UTC-SLS (smeared leap second) time scale. This time scale doesn't contain instants
 * corresponding to leap seconds, but instead "smears" positive and negative leap seconds among the last 1000 seconds
 * of the day when a leap second happens.
 *
 * ## How to acquire
 * - [Clock.now] to query the current moment for a given clock (use [Clock.System] for the platform clock).
 * - [parse] to parse an ISO-8601 string with an offset (for example, `2010-06-01T22:19:44.475Z`).
 * - [fromEpochMilliseconds] / [fromEpochSeconds] to build from Unix-epoch based timestamps.
 *
 * ## How to use
 * - Convert to a local representation for display/UI via [toLocalDateTime] and a [TimeZone].
 * - Store or transmit timestamps as an [Instant] (or as epoch milliseconds/seconds) when you mean a concrete moment.
 *
 * Basic example:
 * ```kotlin
 * val now: Instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val local: LocalDateTime = now.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
 * ```
 *
 * ## Conversion rules and recommendations
 * - Adding a [kotlin.time.Duration] to an [Instant] is always a physical-time operation.
 * - Calendar-based arithmetic (adding days/months/years) depends on time zone rules and therefore requires a
 *   [TimeZone] (see [Instant.plus] with [DateTimeUnit] or [DateTimePeriod]).
 */"}

{"file_path":"/project/core/common/src/LocalDateTime.kt","old_string":"/**
 * The representation of a specific civil date and time without a reference to a particular time zone.
 *
 * This class does not describe specific *moments in time*, which are represented as [Instant] values.
 * Instead, its instances can be thought of as clock readings, something that an observer in a particular time zone
 * could witness.
 * For example, `2020-08-30T18:43` is not a *moment in time*, since someone in Berlin and someone in Tokyo would witness
 * this on their clocks at different times.
 *
 * The main purpose of this class is to provide human-readable representations of [Instant] values, or to transfer them
 * as data.
 *
 * The arithmetic on [LocalDateTime] values is not provided, since without accounting for the time zone transitions it may give misleading results.
 */","new_string":"/**
 * A civil date and time without a time zone.
 *
 * [LocalDateTime] is a set of calendar components (year, month, day, hour, â€¦) and does not represent a unique moment
 * on the time axis. It can be thought of as a "clock reading" that an observer in *some* time zone could witness.
 * For example, `2020-08-30T18:43` is not a moment in time: it will correspond to different [Instant] values in Berlin
 * and in Tokyo.
 *
 * ## How to acquire
 * - Convert an [Instant] to local components using [Instant.toLocalDateTime] and a [TimeZone].
 * - Parse an ISO-8601 local date-time string using [parse].
 * - Construct from components using the constructors.
 *
 * ## How to use
 * Use [LocalDateTime] when the *local* date and time matter and the time zone is known separately:
 * scheduled events, reminders, human-entered values, and UI state.
 *
 * If you need a globally unambiguous timestamp, use [Instant] instead.
 *
 * ## Interaction with [TimeZone] and conversion rules
 * Converting a [LocalDateTime] to an [Instant] requires choosing a [TimeZone]. The mapping may be ambiguous or
 * impossible during time zone transitions (for example, daylight-saving time overlaps/gaps). The conversion rules are
 * defined in [LocalDateTime.toInstant].
 */"}

{"file_path":"/project/core/common/src/TimeZone.kt","old_string":"/**
 * A time zone, provides the conversion between [Instant] and [LocalDateTime] values
 * using a collection of rules specifying which [LocalDateTime] value corresponds to each [Instant].
 */","new_string":"/**
 * A time zone.
 *
 * [TimeZone] provides rules for converting between a physical time point ([Instant]) and a local, human calendar
 * representation ([LocalDateTime]).
 *
 * The library is based on ISO-8601 / the proleptic Gregorian calendar for calendar fields.
 * Region-based time zones are typically identified using IANA Time Zone Database (TZDB) identifiers such as
 * `Europe/Berlin`.
 *
 * ## How to acquire
 * - [currentSystemDefault] to query the system time zone.
 * - [of] to obtain a time zone by its string identifier.
 * - [UTC] for the fixed UTC offset.
 * - For a known fixed offset, use [FixedOffsetTimeZone] or [UtcOffset.asTimeZone].
 *
 * ## How to use
 * The most common operation is conversion:
 * ```kotlin
 * val now: Instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val systemTz = TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()
 * val local: LocalDateTime = now.toLocalDateTime(systemTz)
 * val back: Instant = local.toInstant(systemTz)
 * ```
 *
 * ## Conversion rules and gotchas
 * Converting from [Instant] to [LocalDateTime] is unambiguous.
 * Converting from [LocalDateTime] to [Instant] can be ambiguous or impossible during time zone transitions
 * (for example, daylight-saving time gaps/overlaps). See [LocalDateTime.toInstant] for the resolution rules.
 *
 * Time zone rules are subject to change by platform and by updates to time zone databases.
 */"}

{"file_path":"/project/core/common/src/TimeZone.kt","old_string":"/**
 * A time zone that is known to always have the same offset from UTC.
 */","new_string":"/**
 * A time zone that is known to always have the same offset from UTC.
 *
 * Unlike region-based [TimeZone] implementations, a [FixedOffsetTimeZone] has no daylight-saving transitions and no
 * historical rule changes: the offset is constant for all instants.
 *
 * ## How to acquire
 * - Use [TimeZone.UTC] for `Z` (UTC+0).
 * - Construct directly with [FixedOffsetTimeZone]`(offset)`.
 * - Convert a [UtcOffset] to a time zone with [UtcOffset.asTimeZone].
 * - Use [TimeZone.of] with an offset identifier like `+02:00` or `UTC-3`.
 *
 * ## When to use
 * Prefer [FixedOffsetTimeZone] when you explicitly want a constant offset and do not need region-based rules.
 */"}

{"file_path":"/project/core/common/src/Clock.kt","old_string":"/**
 * A source of [Instant] values.
 *
 * See [Clock.System][Clock.System] for the clock instance that queries the operating system.
 */","new_string":"/**
 * A source of [Instant] values.
 *
 * [Clock] is the entry point for obtaining the current moment of time as an [Instant].
 *
 * ## How to acquire
 * - Use [Clock.System] for the platform clock.
 * - Accept a [Clock] as a dependency to make code deterministic and testable.
 *
 * ## Contract and platform specifics
 * The values produced by [now] are *wall-clock* time as provided by the platform.
 * This clock is not guaranteed to be monotonic: it may jump forwards or backwards if the system time is adjusted
 * (for example, due to user action or time synchronization).
 *
 * If you need to measure elapsed time rather than obtain a timestamp, prefer [asTimeSource] and
 * [kotlin.time.TimeSource] instead.
 *
 * ## Basic usage
 * ```kotlin
 * val current: Instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val today: LocalDate = Clock.System.todayIn(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
 * ```
 */"}

tool_calls.input_json:
{"file_path":"/project/core/common/src/TimeZone.kt","old_string":"/**
 * A time zone, provides the conversion between [Instant] and [LocalDateTime] values
 * using a collection of rules specifying which [LocalDateTime] value corresponds to each [Instant].
 */","new_string":"/**
 * A time zone.
 *
 * [TimeZone] provides rules for converting between a physical time point ([Instant]) and a local, human calendar
 * representation ([LocalDateTime]).
 *
 * The library is based on ISO-8601 / the proleptic Gregorian calendar for calendar fields.
 * Region-based time zones are typically identified using IANA Time Zone Database (TZDB) identifiers such as
 * `Europe/Berlin`.
 *
 * ## How to acquire
 * - [currentSystemDefault] to query the system time zone.
 * - [of] to obtain a time zone by its string identifier.
 * - [UTC] for the fixed UTC offset.
 * - For a known fixed offset, use [FixedOffsetTimeZone] or [UtcOffset.asTimeZone].
 *
 * ## How to use
 * The most common operation is conversion:
 * ```kotlin
 * val now: Instant = Clock.System.now()
 * val systemTz = TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()
 * val local: LocalDateTime = now.toLocalDateTime(systemTz)
 * val back: Instant = local.toInstant(systemTz)
 * ```
 *
 * ## Conversion rules and gotchas
 * Converting from [Instant] to [LocalDateTime] is unambiguous.
 * Converting from [LocalDateTime] to [Instant] can be ambiguous or impossible during time zone transitions
 * (for example, daylight-saving time gaps/overlaps). See [LocalDateTime.toInstant] for the resolution rules.
 *
 * Time zone rules are subject to change by platform and by updates to time zone databases.
 *
 * ## Platform integrations
 * Some targets provide additional conversion utilities to native time zone types (for example, `java.time.ZoneId`
 * on the JVM and `NSTimeZone` on Darwin). These are declared in platform-specific source sets.
 */"}

 14â†’/**
 15â†’ * A time zone.

 18â†’ * representation ([LocalDateTime]).

 20â†’ * The library is based on ISO-8601 / the proleptic Gregorian calendar for calendar fields.
 21â†’ * Region-based time zones are typically identified using IANA Time Zone Database (TZDB) identifiers such as
 22â†’ * `Europe/Berlin`.
 23â†’ *
 24â†’ * ## How to acquire
 25â†’ * - [currentSystemDefault] to query the system time zone.
 26â†’ * - [of] to obtain a time zone by its string identifier.
 27â†’ * - [UTC] for the fixed UTC offset.
 28â†’ * - For a known fixed offset, use [FixedOffsetTimeZone] or [UtcOffset.asTimeZone].
 29â†’ *
 30â†’ * ## How to use
 31â†’ * The most common operation is conversion:
 32â†’ * ```kotlin
 33â†’ * val now: Instant = Clock.System.now()
 34â†’ * val systemTz = TimeZone.currentSystemDefault()
 35â†’ * val local: LocalDateTime = now.toLocalDateTime(systemTz)
 36â†’ * val back: Instant = local.toInstant(systemTz)
 37â†’ * ```
 38â†’ *
 39â†’ * ## Conversion rules and gotchas
 40â†’ * Converting from [Instant] to [LocalDateTime] is unambiguous.
 41â†’ * Converting from [LocalDateTime] to [Instant] can be ambiguous or impossible during time zone transitions
 42â†’ * (for example, daylight-saving time gaps/overlaps). See [LocalDateTime.toInstant] for the resolution rules.
 43â†’ *
 44â†’ * Time zone rules are subject to change by platform and by updates to time zone databases.
 45â†’ *
 46â†’ * ## Platform integrations
 47â†’ * Some targets provide additional conversion utilities to native time zone types (for example, `java.time.ZoneId`
 48â†’ * on the JVM and `NSTimeZone` on Darwin). These are declared in platform-specific source sets.
 49â†’ */

 56â†’     */

 58â†’
 59â†’    // TODO: Declare and document toString/equals/hashCode
 60â†’

 62â†’        /**

 64â†’         *

 66â†’         */

 68â†’
 69â†’        /**
 70â†’         * Returns the time zone with the fixed UTC+0 offset.
 71â†’         */

 73â†’

 79â†’         * - a string starting with '+', '-', `UTC+`, `UTC-`, `UT+`, `UT-`, `GMT+`, `GMT-` â€” identifiers the time zone

 90â†’        /**

14â†’/**
15â†’ * A moment in time.
16â†’ *
17â†’ * [Instant] represents a single point on the time axis, independent of any time zone.
18â†’ * For example, `1970-01-01T00:00:00` *without* specifying a time zone is not a moment in time (it is a
19â†’ * [LocalDateTime]), because observers in different zones will see that clock reading at different moments.
20â†’ * In contrast, `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` or `1970-01-01T00:00:00+00:00` denotes a specific moment and can be represented
21â†’ * as an [Instant].
22â†’ *
23â†’ * ## Time scale
24â†’ * `Instant` uses the UTC-SLS (smeared leap second) time scale. This time scale doesn't contain instants
25â†’ * corresponding to leap seconds, but instead "smears" positive and negative leap seconds among the last 1000 seconds
26â†’ * of the day when a leap second happens.
27â†’ *
28â†’ * ## How to acquire
29â†’ * - [Clock.now] to query the current moment for a given clock (use [Clock.System] for the platform clock).
30â†’ * - [parse] to parse an ISO-8601 string with an offset (for example, `2010-06-01T22:19:44.475Z`).
31â†’ * - [fromEpochMilliseconds] / [fromEpochSeconds] to build from Unix-epoch based timestamps.
32â†’ *
33â†’ * ## How to use
34â†’ * - Convert to a local representation for display/UI via [toLocalDateTime] and a [TimeZone].
35â†’ * - Store or transmit timestamps as an [Instant] (or as epoch milliseconds/seconds) when you mean a concrete moment.
36â†’ *
37â†’ * Basic example:
38â†’ * ```kotlin
39â†’ * val now: Instant = Clock.System.now()
40â†’ * val local: LocalDateTime = now.toLocalDateTime(TimeZone.currentSystemDefault())
41â†’ * ```
42â†’ *
43â†’ * ## Platform integrations
44â†’ * Some targets provide additional conversion utilities to native date/time types (for example, `java.time.Instant`
45â†’ * on the JVM, `Date` on JS, and `NSDate` on Darwin). These are declared in platform-specific source sets.
46â†’ *
47â†’ * ## Conversion rules and recommendations
48â†’* - Adding a [kotlin.time.Duration] to an [Instant] is always a physical-time operation.
49â†’ * - Calendar-based arithmetic (adding days/months/years) depends on time zone rules and therefore requires a
50â†’ *   [TimeZone] (see [Instant.plus] with [DateTimeUnit] or [DateTimePeriod]).
51â†’ */
52â†’@Serializable(with = InstantIso8601Serializer::class)
53â†’public expect class Instant : Comparable<Instant> {
54â†’
55â†’    /**
56â†’     * The number of seconds from the epoch instant `1970-01-01T00:00:00Z` rounded down to a [Long] number.
57â†’     *
58â†’     * The difference between the rounded number of seconds and the actual number of seconds
59â†’     * is returned by [nanosecondsOfSecond] property expressed in nanoseconds.
60â†’     *
61â†’     * Note that this number doesn't include leap seconds added or removed since the epoch.
62â†’     *
63â†’     * @see Instant.fromEpochSeconds
64â†’     */
65â†’    public val epochSeconds: Long
66â†’
67â†’    /**
68â†’     * The number of nanoseconds by which this instant is later than [epochSeconds] from the epoch instant.
69â†’     *
70â†’     * The value is always positive and lies in the range `0..999_999_999`.

13â†’/**
14â†’ * A civil date and time without a time zone.
15â†’ *
16â†’ * [LocalDateTime] is a set of calendar components (year, month, day, hour, â€¦) and does not represent a unique moment
17â†’ * on the time axis. It can be thought of as a "clock reading" that an observer in *some* time zone could witness.
18â†’ * For example, `2020-08-30T18:43` is not a moment in time: it will correspond to different [Instant] values in Berlin
19â†’ * and in Tokyo.
20â†’ *
21â†’ * ## How to acquire
22â†’ * - Convert an [Instant] to local components using [Instant.toLocalDateTime] and a [TimeZone].
23â†’ * - Parse an ISO-8601 local date-time string using [parse].
24â†’ * - Construct from components using the constructors.
25â†’ *
26â†’ * ## How to use
27â†’ * Use [LocalDateTime] when the *local* date and time matter and the time zone is known separately:
28â†’ * scheduled events, reminders, human-entered values, and UI state.
29â†’ *
30â†’ * The default string representation (see [toString] and [parse]) uses an ISO-8601 local date-time format
31â†’ * without an offset.
32â†’ *
33â†’ * If you need a globally unambiguous timestamp, use [Instant] instead.
34â†’ *
35â†’ * ## Interaction with [TimeZone] and conversion rules
36â†’ * Converting a [LocalDateTime] to an [Instant] requires choosing a [TimeZone]. The mapping may be ambiguous or
37â†’* impossible during time zone transitions (for example, daylight-saving time overlaps/gaps). The conversion rules are
38â†’ * defined in [LocalDateTime.toInstant].
39â†’ */
